Canada's big pensions are ready for airport privatization; airports hold 'scarcity value'; Infrastructure; Working group led by Poloz looks at freeing up Canadian portals

Author: Shecter, Barbara

Publication info: Shecter, Barbara. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/canadas-big-pensions-are-ready-airport/docview/3096589011/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=Canada%27s+big+pensions+are+ready+for+airport+privatization%3B+airports+hold+%27scarcity+value%27%3B+Infrastructure%3B+Working+group+led+by+Poloz+looks+at+freeing+up+Canadian+portals&amp;title=National+Post&amp;issn=14868008&amp;date=2024-08-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=FP.1&amp;au=Shecter%2C+Barbara&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The check-in area of London City Airport, about a 30-minute Tube ride from Canary Wharf, looks much like any mid-sized hub in North America. There are queues of passengers waiting to check bags, busy shops and restaurants, and a duty-free shop.
But unlike virtually all North American airports, London City, which now flies more than 3.4 million passengers per year to destinations from the French Alps to Ibiza, isn't owned by a local government, a not-for-profit entity or even a British company.
Rather, it's majority owned by some of Canada's largest pension plans, which snapped up their stake for about two billion pounds in 2016 as part of a consortium that includes the infrastructure arm of the Kuwait Investment Authority.
Since the Ontario Teachers'Pension Plan, Alberta Investment Management Corp. (AIMCo) and Ontario Municipal Employees Retirement System (OMERS) got on board, London City has spent tens of millions of pounds to build new aircraft berths and a parallel taxiway, and completed a major upgrade of its departure lounge, boosting its capacity by 30 per cent.
In 2021, it replaced its aging 30-yearold analog air traffic control tower with a fully remote digital tower, a first for a significant international airport. And the adoption of new scanning technology has reduced lineup times for passengers by 50 per cent on average, with the airport boasting in an April 2024 news release that it can get business and leisure passengers "from the entrance to the gate in 10 minutes."
While the airport has clashed with nearby residents and local governments over some of its expansion plans, it has racked up accolades from travellers, including being named the best airport in the United Kingdom by Condé Nast Traveller readers and the Daily Telegraph in 2022.
Kate Miller, a Canadian-born public relations specialist now living in London, said she had no idea Canadian pensions owned the airport she has chosen for recent trips. But the Mc-Gill University-educated Miller, whose frequent flights have also taken her to Billy Bishop Toronto City Airport and Montréal-Trudeau International Airport, did notice improvements at London City Airport.
"The highlight of the airport is the seamless security system," she said, adding that travellers no longer have to remove liquids or laptops from their bags or take off their shoes like they do at other large airports around the world.
London City is not the only airport Canadian pensions have taken stakes in over the years. From Sydney to Copenhagen, airports are an investment opportunity they desire, but one they have been unable to replicate in their home market, where a unique not-forprofit ownership model and fears of a public-to-private shift would be a losing political move have stymied previous talks about privatizing.
That might be about to change. A working group - led by former Bank of Canada governor Stephen Poloz - to identify pension fund investment opportunities across several sectors, including airports, was introduced in the federal government's spring budget.
Poloz has already received submissions and is meeting informally with the pension funds and other stakeholders before laying out the next steps, according to sources familiar with the process.
If the airports become available, the pensions, as they have done before, will be lining up to buy. The big question is whether the government - and Canadian travellers - are ready to take the plunge.
'RRBs ON STEROIDS'
The allure of airports for the pensions is twofold. On one hand, there are the healthy fees collected each time an airplane takes off and lands, not to mention a surrounding near-monopoly constellation of amenities - such as parking and rental cars - that offer steady earnings potential and growth opportunities.
They also help the retirement funds satisfy long-term obligations to pensioners. Airports are infrastructure investments, an asset class whose steady returns keep up with inflation and help pensions match their liabilities over the long term.
One industry veteran said airport investments are like souped-up real return bonds (RRBs), an asset class gobbled up by pension funds specifically because it matches liabilities - what's owed to future pensioners - that are indexed to inflation.
"They are RRBs on steroids," said Jim Leech, who was chief executive of the Ontario Teachers'Pension Plan from 2007 until 2014, a period during which it made several investments in airports.
It began with an initial investment in Bristol and Birmingham airports in the United Kingdom in 2001, when Leech was running the pension's private investment group, and grew to include a portfolio of stakes in five airports across Europe and the U.K.: Copenhagen Airport, Brussels Airport, London City Airport, which was purchased from private equity fund Global Infrastructure Partners, full ownership of Bristol Airport and 45 per cent ownership of Birmingham International Airport.
Teachers', Canada's largest single-profession pension plan, also dipped a toe into Australia's privatized airports, buying an 11 per cent stake in Sydney Airport, but that stake was ultimately swapped out in 2011 for minority stakes in airports in Brussels and Copenhagen.
"Airports performed well while I was at Teachers," Leech said. "Returns were on par with other infrastructure investments."
The investments were good for the airports, too, he added.
Long-term financial backing from institutional investors like pensions gives airports the means to expand capacity and develop ancillary revenue streams, such as from retail, at a pace and scale that can't be matched by governments and not-forprofit airport operators such as those in Canada, he said.
There is also an incentive to find profit through efficiencies - from luggage handling to passenger movement - and bring in more passengers. And there can be real estate development potential for the land that surrounds airports, something much less likely to be exploited by government owners.
Other Canadian pension funds sensed the same opportunity in airport ownership, putting their investment dollars in countries where privatization - and deals among institutional investors - took place.
In 2006, the Caisse de dépôt et placement du Québec joined a consortium that bid $24.7 billion for London's prized Heathrow Airport. And while the Caisse has gradually sold down its position over the years, it retains a stake of around 12 per cent that was valued by the fund manager at more than $1.5 billion in December.
Canadian pensions are far from the only institutional investors to spot the opportunity. Nearly 20 per cent of airports worldwide are now privately owned and more than 100 of those are owned by private equity infrastructure investors, according to a 2023 study published by the United States National Bureau of Economic Research.
None are in Canada, and there is only one - Missouri's Branson Airport - in the U.S. Most airports in the U.S. remain in state or local government hands, a situation often attributed to the unique tax-exempt treatment of bonds issued to finance public airports.
A MUCH BETTER MODEL
Tae Hoon Oum, a retired professor at the University of British Columbia's A plan by London City Airport to extend the hours flights can take off and land and increase the regulated passenger cap was rejected by local government officials in July 2023 amid noise and air quality concerns. passengers. And there can be real estate development potential for the land that surrounds airports, something much less likely to be exploited by government owners.
Other Canadian pension funds sensed the same opportunity in airport ownership, putting their investment dollars in countries where privatization - and deals among institutional investors - took place.
In 2006, the Caisse de dépôt et placement du Québec joined a consortium that bid $24.7 billion for London's prized Heathrow Airport. And while the Caisse has gradually sold down its position over the years, it retains a stake of around 12 per cent that was valued by the fund manager at more than $1.5 billion in December.
Canadian pensions are far from the only institutional investors to spot the opportunity. Nearly 20 per cent of airports worldwide are now privately owned and more than 100 of those are owned by private equity infrastructure investors, according to a 2023 study published by the United States National Bureau of Economic Research.
None are in Canada, and there is only one - Missouri's Branson Airport - in the U.S. Most airports in the U.S. remain in state or local government hands, a situation often attributed to the unique tax-exempt treatment of bonds issued to finance public airports.
A MUCH BETTER MODEL
Tae Hoon Oum, a retired professor at the University of British Columbia's Sauder School of Business who helped create an international database to track airport performance and cost competitiveness, said Canada is late to the party when it comes to recognizing the benefits of privatizing airport operations.
"The world has moved to a much better model. People who know airports, how it works in the airport industry, they know that we are falling behind," he said, noting that airports in Australia were privatized by the early 2000s and that governments in Europe and the U.K. privatized airport operations as far back as the late 1980s and 1990s.
With air travel growing at a steady five per cent each year on average, the appetite is certainly there for infrastructure investors, including Canadian pensions, to continue to gobble them up, Oum said. Major airports in Toronto, Montreal, Calgary and Vancouver would be the most sensible places to start if a decision is made to privatize them.
The Canada Pension Plan Investment Board (CPPIB) has made no secret of its desire to add airports to its hefty infrastructure portfolio, one of the few that does not include any major airports. Now, that seems tantalizingly within reach.
"We have not invested in airports as much as some of our peers around the world, (but) it's not due to a lack of interest," John Graham, head of the management organization for the country's largest pension fund, said not long after the federal budget was unveiled in Ottawa.
He said the search continues and he did not rule out interest in Canada as Oum said. Major airports in Toronto, Montreal, Calgary and Vancouver would be the most sensible places to start if a decision is made to privatize them.
The Canada Pension Plan Investment Board (CPPIB) has made no secret of its desire to add airports to its hefty infrastructure portfolio, one of the few that does not include any major airports. Now, that seems tantalizingly within reach.
"We have not invested in airports as much as some of our peers around the world, (but) it's not due to a lack of interest," John Graham, head of the management organization for the country's largest pension fund, said not long after the federal budget was unveiled in Ottawa.
He said the search continues and he did not rule out interest in Canada as the government dangles the prospect of airport sales for the first time since 2016.
"We'll continue to look at assets that become available," Graham said. "Airports are interesting to big institutional investors because there's a scarcity value to them."
An airport usually has a monopoly in the region in which they operate, plus an ecosystem of ancillary businesses, from parking to retail facilities, which adds to the business case, he said. There are also adjacent lands that can be developed.
"From an investment perspective, there's a lot of interesting components to it," Graham said.
It's not always an easy process, however. Privatization around the world has faced resistance, including from consumers who fear that turning the operations over to for-profit operators will result in overdevelopment, noisier skies, higher fees and fewer workers. As a result, airports typically remain highly regulated even after privatization. Even once an airport is privatized, efforts to increase traffic and expand operations can face resistance.
For example, a plan by London City Airport to extend the hours that flights can take off and land and increase the regulated passenger cap by nearly 40 per cent - despite not yet returning to pre-pandemic levels - was rejected by local government officials in July 2023 amid community concerns over air quality and noise, among other things.
The Canadian pen-sion-controlled airport, which had already worked with airlines to ensure that planes taking off and landing during expanded hours were only cleaner, quieter, new-generation aircraft, has appealed the ruling.
Michael Weisbach, a finance professor at Ohio State University and co-author of the National Bureau of Economic Research study that found that airport volumes, efficiency and quality improve substantially under private equity ownership, said resistance to privatization is nevertheless a common response.
"When you first hear about privatizing airports, your first reaction is, 'Oh, my god, they're going to fire a lot of workers, they're going to compromise on safety,'" he said. "We looked as hard as we could to see whether there was any increase in safety violations or increase in accidents, or whether there were fewer employees, and we didn't find any evidence of any of that."
Moreover, the study concluded that the net effect of a private equity acquisition of an airport is a rough doubling of operating income, mostly due to higher revenues collected from airlines and retailers in the terminal rather than cost-cutting. Other things that improved under new private equity management included better compensation for managers and more investments in new capacity and technology.
"The facts are that the number of routes and the number of passengers go up, the profitability goes up, the passenger experience goes up," Weisbach said, adding that "the cancellation rate goes down (and) safety is unaffected."
Canadian pensions are unique in the way they invest in global airports, with many other pensions getting only indirect exposure by investing through private equity funds, he said, but the same results apply.
Earlier studies have produced mixed results, but the National Bureau of Economic Research study, which used a database of 2,444 unique airports in 217 countries and had rare access to some private equity financial data, found stark differences in passenger traffic patterns, which increased more under private ownership than at government-owned airports. Private equity owners far outperformed even other forms of privatization on this measure.
Satellite images showed that private equity owners tend to increase terminal size and the number of gates. Investments in such physical capacity mean an airport can bring in more passengers, who, in turn, spend more money in retail establishments and restaurants and on parking and rental cars.
The study's authors also noted that private equity owners tend to push for deregulation of government limits on fees. In one example cited, price caps at three Australian airports that were privatized in the mid-1990s were replaced by a system of monitoring that lets the government intervene only if fees or revenues become excessive.
Weisbach remembers the first time he flew into Sweden and felt pretty sure he was in a government-owned airport without any knowledge beforehand. After a 45-minute wait in a line snaking through the terminal, he drew the ire of everyone behind him while he frantically searched for a document he was unaware he needed to present once he reached the front of the line.
"It turns out that they had one guy open (and) I got everyone in line mad at me because I was searching my phone trying to find my exit ticket," he recalled, adding that when he was finally through, he quizzed a Swedish friend to see if his assumption about the airport's ownership was correct.
"I said, 'You must not have a privatized airport.'And he said, 'No, no, no, it's awful. We wish we had a privatized airport, because this kind of thing just would never happen.'" WHAT A SALE MIGHT LOOK LIKE
Airport ownership in Canada is unique: the federal government has continued to own the land, acting as the landlord after turning operations of 21 of the largest airports over to private, not-for-profit airport authority organizations in the 1990s.
Each airport authority pays rent to the federal government, calculated as a percentage of the gross revenue earned, up to a total of 12 per cent. Airport rents collected by Ottawa totalled $6.5 billion by 2019, according to the Canadian Airports Council. All retained revenue must be reinvested in the airport. A Crown corporation retains security functions.
Justin Trudeau's Liberal government toyed with the idea of privatizing airports to be controlled by for-profit entities - either partly or fully - shortly after being elected to power in 2015, a process started by his Conservative predecessor, Stephen Harper.
The government hired investment bank Credit Suisse Group AG to study the benefits of privatization, and estimates at the time from the C.D. Howe Institute pegged the value of Toronto's Pearson International Airport and seven others at $17 billion. Pension executives were on record lining up to buy. But sources familiar with the process say the Liberals got cold feet because they feared turning airport operations into a for-profit venture would cost them votes.
Notably, the Liberal party has had a history of objecting to private interests in Canada's airports. In the early 1990s, Jean Chrétien, while in opposition, called a Conservative government's attempt to push through a long-term plan to involve private interests in the redevelopment of two terminals at Pearson airport - just ahead of an election - "indecent" and "immoral," and he threatened to overturn it if he was elected.
But times have changed.
Finance Minister Chrystia Freeland floated the idea of forcing Canada's pensions to invest more of their billions of dollars at home in last year's fiscal update, which was met with an angry backlash. In this spring's budget, the government seemed to be offering the pensions something they had long wanted: the potential to invest in domestic infrastructure at a scale large enough to capture their interest and with steady profit potential from the get-go.
Another recent development that may influence the privatization debate is that Canada's main airport authorities took a beating along with the rest of the travel industry when travel was grounded by the COVID-19 pandemic in 2020.
While losing around 75 per cent of their passenger traffic that year and the following one, the country's already indebted airport authorities also took on an additional $2.8 billion in debt, according to the Canadian Airports Council.
Airport infrastructure investment lagged during the pandemic and in its aftermath, the Centre for Aviation, a market intelligence group, said in a spring report on Canada's revived debate around airport privatization.
"That means now is a good time to consider if it is the time to resurrect the airport privatization study," the May 8 report said.
It is a financial picture that industry sources say could make airport operations more enticing to institutional investors because more debt against the same value would lower the cost of buying equity.
Dale Burgess, who leads the Infrastructure and Natural Resources department at the Ontario Teachers'Pension Plan, said he was not prepared to discuss whether the after-effects of the pandemic make a potential Canadian airport acquisition more appealing from a financial perspective. But he said the "patient" capital and financial wherewithal offered by pension funds have helped airports they own around the world manage through tough times.
"In situations like COVID, that's where having a longterm perspective as a pension fund investor really has come to bear," he said. "We, for example, provide a lot of support to our existing airports as a steward of these assets and own them for a long period of time, (making) sure that we provide support from a management perspective, support from a capital perspective, support from a financing perspective as well."
Each airport is different and Teachers'created a subsidiary, Ontario Airports Investments, to manage and oversee its airport investments across Europe. Such expertise could come in handy as the Canadian government, advised by Poloz, considers whether and how to privatize.
"It will be situation-specific," said Leech, the former Teachers' CEO. "An institution may determine to assume the current capital structure, or may want to ... raise its own debt. Most likely, government will retain land ownership and award a multi-year concession license to own/operate the airport."
He added that foreign ownership should be encouraged to maximize returns, noting that any interested institutional investors would be likely to take on Canadian partners.
Several options for Canada's airports were laid out in 2016 as part of former Conservative cabinet minister David Emerson's comprehensive review of the Canada Transport Act, which included the potential for the government to maintain full or partial land ownership while privatizing airport operations.
If the operations were sold to private interests, it would involve creating a new concession or management contract and implementing legislation to dissolve the existing leases and airport authorities, the report said.
Another option would be to form an equity partnership with a private-sector manager, which would own at least 49 per cent of the airport and be responsible for its operation under a contract. This, again, would require legislation to dissolve the existing leases and airport authorities.
Emerson's report noted that Birmingham International Airport in the U.K. operated under a similar model, with the Teachers'as a minority stakeholder.
In yet another scenario, the existing airport authorities could be converted into a share capital, for-profit business corporation under the Canada Business Corporations Act and the airport lands could be sold to the authorities. This would require legislation to continue or transfer assets and liabilities. In some cases, it would also require working with the provinces and First Nations to manage provincial legislation and outstanding land claims.
The final proposal was to dissolve existing leases and airport authorities and sell the assets directly to a private company or consortium of investors.
"Up to 100 per cent ownership of the airports could be offered in one sale, as was done with the large airports in the United Kingdom in the 1980s," the report said. "Or the sale could be completed in tranches over a period of time, as has been done in the past with the sale of Canadian Crown corporations like Petro-Canada and Air Canada."
'IT'S GOING TO TAKE SOME TIME'
If the process being led by Poloz kickstarts one of those options, or generates a new plan to shake up Canada's skies, it is likely to require a lengthy consultation with stakeholders and legislative change.
"It's not going to be as simple as a stroke of a pen," said John Gradek, a veteran airline manager and programs coordinator of integrated aviation management at McGill University. "It's going to take some time."
He said that while the brief debate prompted by the Emerson report eight years ago ended with the status quo, it left the door open if circumstances changed, which he believes they have.
"The 1990s dialogue on not-for-profit entities for Canadian airports has been superseded by the needs of airports to get massive funding to maintain and expand their infrastructure," he said. "The not-for-profit model doesn't fit very well in organizations that need to optimize their capital spend."
Pearson, Canada's main international airport, had the dubious honour of making headlines in the summer of 2022 for having the world's most flight delays and was ranked the secondworst "mega airport" in North America for overall traveller satisfaction by J.D. Power in results published last year.
There have, of course, been alternatives to airport privatization offered up to improve the operation of Canadian aeronautical operations and make ticket prices more competitive.
For example, the Canadian Airports Council, a group representing more than 100 airports across Canada that handle 90 per cent of domestic passenger traffic, has pushed for the federal government to invest the rent it collects from airports into the infrastructure that supports the operations. This would remove such costs for the airport authorities that must now be funded, in part, by passengers through higher ticket prices.
The council issued a statement after the Poloz working group was formed that said it hoped the process would address the airport association's request to the government for "more financial investment tools" to meet the growing air travel and business development needs.
All this is taking place against the backdrop of a probe by the federal competition watchdog into ticket prices and airline competition amid a growing number of customer complaints and the collapse of domestic discount airlines Lynx Air and Swoop, as well as the recent filing for creditor protection of Canada Jetlines Ltd. However, the Competition Bureau has indicated it does not plan to look at airport governance "except to the extent that they relate to competition in domestic air passenger services."
Pension executives from the Caisse, OMERS and AIMCo declined interviews to discuss the possibility of airport ownership in Canada, with some sources suggesting their reluctance may be driven by concern about jeopardizing the process and how far Trudeau is willing to go this time. Some are expecting Poloz to lay out the next steps as soon as this month.
Oum, whose bona fides include being hired as a market expert by Australia's competition bureau when it was weighing the potential merger of two major airlines in 2004, said the post-pandemic landscape combined with a renewed focus on Canada's lagging productivity relative to other G7 countries could help make airport privatization a federal election issue.
"I think this may be something to watch out (for) before the next year's election," he said, adding that a move toward privatization might win favour with provincial and municipal governments that stand to share potential profits.
"It may be the right time."
Financial Post bshecter@postmedia.com

Document 355 of 500

Harris-Walz a golden gift for Trump; Never-Trumpers may have cause for a rethink

Author: Taube, Michael

Publication info: Taube, Michael. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/harris-walz-golden-gift-trump-never-trumpers-may/docview/3096588921/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=Harris-Walz+a+golden+gift+for+Trump%3B+Never-Trumpers+may+have+cause+for+a+rethink&amp;title=National+Post&amp;issn=14868008&amp;date=2024-08-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.15&amp;au=Taube%2C+Michael&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: When Donald Trump went from being a high-profile businessman and reality TV star to Republican presidential candidate and winning the White House in 2016, some on the right opposed him. There is still a contingent of right-leaning individuals who form the "Never Trump movement." Largely composed of moderate conservatives and Republicans, they have consistently stood against Trump and said they would never support him or his presidency.
Yet, for the first time in nearly a decade, there's a real possibility this lifetime political ban could come crashing down. It's entirely conceivable that the Democratic ticket of Vice-President Kamala Harris and Minnesota Gov. Tim Walz could lead some Never Trumpers to do the unthinkable and vote for Trump in November.
This isn't the Democratic party of old. Many Americans had strong differences of opinion with Democratic presidents such as Franklin D. Roosevelt, John F. Kennedy, Bill Clinton and Barack Obama, but they understood where these guys were coming from and what they were trying to do.
Today's Democrats are nearly unrecognizable. The party is more left-wing, radicalized and polarizing than any previous incarnation. The Harris-Walz ticket may very well be the most left-leaning ticket the Democrats have ever put forward in a presidential election. The damage they could potentially do to the U.S. economy and the political process should unnerve American voters, including some Never Trumpers.
Harris fell into the top of the ticket when President Joe Biden dropped out of the race in July. Widely regarded as a political lightweight, the one-term California senator had hopes of becoming the 2020 Democratic presidential nominee but suspended her campaign before the primaries. She wasn't her party's first choice in 2024, but rather its last, desperate hope to save the White House.
Meanwhile, Harris's knowledge of politics and public policy is far too rudimentary for a potential occupant of the White House.
Here's her cringeworthy, grade school-like response to a radio host in March 2022: "Ukraine is a country in Europe. It exists next to another country called Russia. Russia is a bigger country. Russia is a powerful country. Russia decided to invade a smaller country called Ukraine. So basically that's wrong."
She's also said: "We invested an additional $12 billion into community banks because we know community banks are in the community"; and "I think that, to be very honest with you, that I do believe that we should have rightly believed, but we certainly believe, that certain issues are just settled."
If she's a potential leader of the free world, then God help us all.
Harris's political ideology is also more left-wing than Biden's ever was on his worst day. She's pro-abortion, pro-affirmative action, supportive of strict gun-control legislation, a defender of sanctuary cities, believer in restrictive environmental protection laws and one of 10 senators to oppose the United States-Mexico-Canada Agreement because it didn't tackle climate change. She's a progressive's progressive.
Walz has tried to present himself as being slightly more moderate than Harris. He's certainly a better orator and political strategist. Nevertheless, his political views and economic policies are similarly beyond the pale.
He signed the 2023 Protect Reproductive Options Act, which codified access to abortion in Minnesota. He decreed that Minnesota would be required to obtain its energy from wind, solar and various carbon-free sources by 2040. He provided free school meals to every child in his state, irrespective of income - a noble gesture, and an insanely expensive one. He's pro-union, pro-police reform and pro-cannabis legalization.
Walz has also made several bizarre, far-left statements. While working as a high-school teacher in Nebraska in the 1990s, he reportedly said that China's communism system "means that everyone is the same and everyone shares ... the doctor and the construction worker make the same." More recently, he said that, "One person's socialism is another person's neighbourliness."
No one is suggesting Trump doesn't have his own political and personal deficiencies. They're a mile wide and an inch deep. Regardless, his nominal support for Republican and conservative values is far better than the Democratic alternative. Some of his biggest Never Trump critics likely know this deep down, too.
If Never Trumpers still regard themselves as free-market thinkers and economic conservatives, they won't be able to vote for the Harris-Walz ticket. As much as they despise Trump, they know he wouldn't have a sympathetic ear to socialist rhetoric and support farleft policies that will tank the economy and ruin their nation for generations to come. Don't be surprised if some Never Trumpers bite their tongues and ultimately pull the lever to give the man they hate the second presidential term they wanted to avoid.
National Post

Business indexing term: Industry: 92111 :‎ Executive Offices 81394 :‎ Political Organizations

Foot soldiers for Marxism?

Author: Leis, Glen

Publication info: Leis, Glen. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/foot-soldiers-marxism/docview/3096588898/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=Foot+soldiers+for+Marxism%3F&amp;title=National+Post&amp;issn=14868008&amp;date=2024-08-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.13&amp;au=Leis%2C+Glen&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Re: A terror backer's award disgrace - Jesse Kline, Aug. 22; CUPE boss Hahn under fire for 'antisemitic' posts - Tyler Dawson, Aug. 22 We all need to start thinking about the actions of CUPE Vice-President Fred Hahn, anti-Israel activist Charlotte Kates, and anti-Israel politicians like NDP MP Heather Macpherson and Independent Ontario MPP Sarah Jama (who was kicked out of the NDP caucus for antisemitic remarks) as part of a greater effort to completely undermine the Western democratic free market.
The "Global Intifada" chant we've been hearing is a convenient stand-in for "Workers of the world unite." Intolerant zealots have been made into useful foot soldiers for the Marxist cause.
Let us not forget that, despite their distaste for the bourgeoisie, the communists took over Russia after they had worked with democratic forces to overthrow the Romanovs; these "allies" were dealt with later. Today's Marxist revolutionaries, despite their support of LGBTQ and women's rights, cynically ignore the homophobia and misogyny of their "allies" for the sake of the revolution. The revolution is all that matters; they will deal with the web of conflicting alliances later.

Document 357 of 500

The cancel mob is being cancelled

Author: Pankratz, Adam

Publication info: Pankratz, Adam. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/cancel-mob-is-being-cancelled/docview/3096588838/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=The+cancel+mob+is+being+cancelled&amp;title=National+Post&amp;issn=14868008&amp;date=2024-08-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.12&amp;au=Pankratz%2C+Adam&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: On Aug. 6, Ottawa's Capital Pride released a pro-Palestinian statement which likely garnered considerably more attention than its authors imagined it would. The statement vaguely attempted to call out all intolerance, including antisemitism, but could not hide the fact that it was itself decidedly antisemitic, though that irony was obviously lost on Capital Pride.
The reaction from local Jewish groups was understandably swift in their declaration they would give Ottawa Pride a pass. A more surprising reaction came from quarters recently rather acquiescent or silent on many things antisemitic. Ottawa's mayor, the Ottawa-Carleton School board, local hospitals and even the Liberal Party of Canada withdrew from participation in the parade.
The descent of Pride and other allegedly progressive events and institutions into the murky waters of extremist left and activist politics has been underway for a while now and is not unique to Capital Pride (see CU-PE Ontario president Fred Hahn for example). So, in many ways, the journey of Capital Pride to the terminus of antisemitism and support for a region where being gay is punishable by death is not altogether surprising, sad though it may be.
But let us take a step back from this single event and look at the larger picture and the dangers very apparently lurking. Indeed, the reaction to the Capital Pride statement could well turn out to be the canary in the coal mine of backlash against activist extremism and woke politics.
For years it has been de rigueur for politicians of all stripes to participate in Pride. Justin Trudeau and the Liberals often act like they love nothing more than a good Pride march. That Capital Pride is too far gone for the Liberals, who have been especially mealymouthed on support for Israel following the Oct. 7 attacks, and who never miss an opportunity to burnish their progressive credentials, should raise eyebrows everywhere. In particular, it should seriously worry Capital Pride, as well as all "progressive" organizations which embraced the scourge of woke identity politics and allowed fringe elements within their organizations to take control.
However, this should also worry centrist and fair-minded Canadians. Large public organizations and politicians turning away from Pride and its newfound extremism may be a good thing, but that turn can quickly devolve into vicious backlash and retaliation if we are not collectively careful.
Some may argue that many of the organizations first in line for a backlash have had it coming for a while now. After all, these are the organizations and people who hounded anyone who said women couldn't have a penis out of a job, tarred innocent citizens as racist, who thought perhaps all lives did, in fact, matter, and generally created an environment of fear around the most salient cultural issues of the last decade. They deserve it, so goes this logic.
I do not disagree that there are people out there who, in the name of progress, displayed the worst and most vicious of behaviour toward anyone who did not agree with their extreme views. It is to be desired that they face consequences for having made the lives of others miserable or having cost blameless people their jobs. I, at any rate, will not shed a tear nor pity them if and when they finally met the sanction they deserve.
But in my desire to see the leaders of activist cancel culture brought to justice, I cannot help but look at the historical record of public retribution. It does not make for happy reading, as anyone who peruses French history circa 1794 can attest.
Retribution and backlash have never been precisely calibrated, scientific instruments. They are blunt tools, subject to wild swings and abuse by those who wield them. And, historically speaking, those who wield them tend to get a thrill out of revenge and reprisal.
This is the fear I have harboured for a while now: that the inevitable backlash against the insane and destructive scourge of activist identity politics would arrive and, when it came, the perpetrators would discover that they were a minority and, the majority now coming for them was not in a conciliatory mood. While minorities persecuting majorities is bad (as we have seen via cancel culture), a majority persecuting a minority, whatever they may have done, has the potential to be worse.
The most vehement and vocal adherents and actors in the culture wars of the past years have done enormous damage to both institutions and individuals. They have cost people their jobs, reputations and, in some cases, their lives. It is not unnatural to want to see such bad actors harmed as they harmed others. By doing so, however, those of us who have stood against the tidal wave of woke activism which threatened society, risk becoming the beasts we fought so hard to push back.
The Capital Pride debacle demonstrates the societal pendulum is swinging back, my fear is it will bludgeon indiscriminately and plunge us further into extreme societal divides.
National Post Adam Pankratz is a lecturer at the University of British Columbia's Sauder School of Business.

Subject: Antisemitism; Extremism; Activists; Identity politics; Ostracism; Parades

Identifier / keyword: capital; pride; released; statement; garnered; considerably

Document 358 of 500

Inside François legault's epic battle with quebec's anglophones

Author: Trépanier, Antoine

Publication info: Trépanier, Antoine. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/inside-françois-legaults-epic-battle-with-quebecs/docview/3096588338/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=Inside+Fran%26ccedil%3Bois+legault%27s+epic+battle+with+quebec%27s+anglophones&amp;title=National+Post&amp;issn=14868008&amp;date=2024-08-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.7&amp;au=Tr%C3%A9panier%2C+Antoine&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Anglophones in Quebec say Premier François Legault's fight to protect French is leaving them "increasingly worried" that they are being "targeted" by their government and feeling "almost like pariahs" in their home province.
Even one of Legault's former candidates has turned on him.
"I would tell Legault how disappointed I am and how small he is," said Karen Hilchey, in an interview with the National Post.
Originally from Nova Scotia, Hilchey lived in Quebec for four decades, where she learned French, studied, worked as an interpreter, got involved in local associations and got elected in municipal politics. She was also a candidate for François Legault's Coalition Avenir Québec in 2018, a party she believed could bring positive changes to Quebec politics.
She says François Legault convinced her to run in the Liberal fortress of Jacques-Cartier, a riding on Montreal's West Island, where he grew up and where his mother still lives.
It's a riding where 65 per cent of the electorate is English-speaking, just like her and, although she was parachuted into the riding, the party hoped to significantly challenge the Liberal candidate Gregory Kelley.
"I regret it. I have actually felt very stupid getting (Legault) votes throughout the elections," said Hilchey.
She lost in a landslide. But it was what happened next that hurt her the most.
Once in office, Legault established himself as one of the strictest premiers on linguistic issues. Protecting French is his mission, and he won't back down even though his style is heavily contested within English-speaking communities, but also by some francophones and experts.
In June 2022, Bill 96 was adopted, making Quebec officially a francophone province. By using the notwithstanding clause of Canada's constitution, the government is trying to strengthen the use of French in public services.
All communications with the government must be solely in French and government entities "may" communicate in English with persons declared "eligible" and in possession of a certificate of eligibility for instruction in English, which is issued by the education department. In addition, immigrants have six months to learn French and then communicate in this language with the state.
Legault then cut funding for anglophone universities and implemented new rules requiring students coming from outside Quebec, including Canada, to pay higher tuition fees.
"He (Legault) has always given himself the right to be a social linguistic bully in a legal way. This is social linguistic bullying, harassment and discrimination all in one," said Hilchey.
POINTS OF FRICTION In a French interview with the National Post, Éric Girard, the minister of finance who is also responsible for relations with English-speaking Quebecers, didn't want to react to Hilchey's comments, saying that he didn't know her and that she was entitled to her opinions.
"There are points of friction that are amplified. We talk about things that are going less well, but overall, the government has daily interactions with English-speakers that go very well," he said.
A study by the Commissioner of Official Languages of Canada tends to support Girard's argument.
"As we face linguistic tensions in society," Raymond Théberge wrote in a July oped, "English-speaking Quebecers are in large part allies of French in Quebec, and they encourage the promotion of bilingualism among English speakers elsewhere in the country."
"Quebecers, both French-speaking and English-speaking, remind us that there is a foundation of goodwill, a way of living together that they express on a daily basis," he wrote, adding that he feels "a cautious optimism for the future of linguistic relations in Quebec and Canada."
Todd Hoffman, the president of Brauwerk Hoffman Brewery in the bilingual region of Pontiac near the nation's capital, agrees. In a region where Ontario and Quebec are almost integrated, he feels both linguistic communities get along well.
But he fears the government wants to break that good relationship. He recently received three written notices from the Office québécois de la langue française, for not having posted predominantly in French on Face book.
"I speak a little French, and I always have a fully bilingual employee at the brewery. We have francophone and anglophone clients and I take pride in serving them in their language. But I don't have the resources to hire a social media manager," he said in an interview.
Many anglophones say they are fighting the provincial government in their own way. Some of them, who speak fluent French, have said they only speak English when they shop or ask for government services.
Hoffman went a step further. He brewed a special beer, a lager he called "Resist 96," and describes as "a beer for those how resist the control of language." The label is bilingual.
Elected officials told the National Post their offices were swamped by calls and emails from anglophones worried about their services.
Recently, a 31-page directive from the Department of Health mentioned the "exceptional" circumstances in which English is allowed to be spoken in hospitals and other medical facilities.
"The second this whole story started, whether it was emails, phone calls or at the coffee shop, grocery store, people stopped me to talk about this. That was of extreme concern," said Gregory Kelley, who is the Liberal critic for relations with English-speaking Quebecers.
Minister Girard acknowledged that the controversy shouldn't have happened in the first place and that communications "could have been much better."
"Under no circumstances is it necessary to have or present a certificate ... to access health services in English," he said.
But there is a trust issue among the anglophone community that Girard is "fully aware of." Things aren't perfect, he said, adding that it is his role to "improve relationships."
THE 'FRAGILE' FRENCH LANGUAGE Meanwhile, Premier Legault seems to want to protect French at all costs.
"Whatever indicators you use, they are all decreasing. The percentage of people speaking French at home, in public, at work, whatever you take, every five years, it decreases. I think it's a matter of facts," said the premier earlier this week.
Yes, but also no, replies Jean-Pierre Corbeil, a professor of sociology at Laval University. With the arrival of a significant number of immigrants, the French-asfirst-language indicator will inevitably decrease, but English will decline, too. In fact, with 94 per cent of the population that speaks French, it seems the language is more present than ever in the province.
"There is no evidence at the moment that allows us to believe that these measures will really have an effective effect on slowing the decline of French," said Corbeil, who thinks French is "fragile" in Quebec.
However, he notes that English speakers are increasingly isolated by the government, and he fears that tensions between the two linguistic groups could rise.
"I think that the strategies adopted by the government have, in my opinion, contributed to deteriorating their sense of belonging to Quebec society," said Corbeil. "This does not bode well for the future."
Frédéric Bérard, professor of linguistic law at the Université de Montréal, argues that Legault's politics of division are unnecessary in a Quebec where most anglophones have immersed themselves in Quebec culture.
"What the Legault government is doing might have been understandable in the 1950s. But today, you disgust the Anglo-Quebecers, but what have they done to deserve it? This is cheap petty politics," said Bérard.
Speaking to the Post, Girard said he is aware of the criticisms, but did not agree with the notion that the government is engaging in "wedge politics."
"My commitment is non-partisan, it is sincere, thoughtful and it is in the best interests of Quebec," he replied.
'THINGS COULD GET WORSE'
The Quebec Community Groups Network (QCGN), which represents thousands of anglophones across the province, feels this division almost every day. Its president Eva Ludvig criticizes the fact that the community isn't able to meet with Legault to explain their grievances. The last meeting was in February 2019, according to the premier's office.
"You know, many of our people have thrown up their arms that there's no point in discussing with this particular government because they never consult us and are not interested in our perspective," she said.
Gregory Kelley says his constituents are keeping their eyes open, closely monitoring Legault's next moves. Will there be more legislation? Other regulations? He personally doubts it.
"But in all honesty, they keep going further as they try to battle the PQ for a certain portion of the electorate, you know, it's concerning. Things could get worse," he said.
For Karen Hilchey, it's too little, too late. Three years ago, she packed her things and left the province. She now lives in Smithers, B.C.
"I could be a nobody in my own country, in a province that I loved, in the city that I love, she said. And yet, I've got no more rights."
National Post atrepanier@postmedia.com

Document 362 of 500

August 23, 2024 (Page A6)

Document 365 of 500

Michael Taube: Will the Harris-Walz ticket push Never Trumpers to support the Republican?

Author: Kline, Jesse

Publication info: Kline, Jesse. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/michael-taube-will-harris-walz-ticket-push-never/docview/3096521069/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=Michael+Taube%3A+Will+the+Harris-Walz+ticket+push+Never+Trumpers+to+support+the+Republican%3F&amp;title=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-08-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Kline%2C+Jesse&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: When Donald Trump went from being a high-profile businessman and reality TV star to Republican presidential candidate and winning the White House in 2016, some on the right opposed him. There is still a contingent of right-leaning individuals who form the "Never Trump movement." Largely composed of moderate conservatives and Republicans, they have consistently stood against Trump and said they would never support him or his presidency.
Yet, for the first time in nearly a decade, there's a real possibility this lifetime political ban could come crashing down. It's entirely conceivable that the Democratic ticket of Vice-President Kamala Harris and Minnesota Gov. Tim Walz could lead some Never Trumpers to do the unthinkable and vote for Trump in November.
This isn't the Democratic party of old. Many Americans had strong differences of opinion with Democratic presidents like Franklin D. Roosevelt, John F. Kennedy, Bill Clinton and Barack Obama, but they understood where these guys were coming from and what they were trying to do.
Today's Democrats are nearly unrecognizable. The party is more left-wing, radicalized and polarizing than any previous incarnation. The Harris-Walz ticket may very well be the most left-leaning ticket the Democrats have ever put forward in a presidential election. The damage they could potentially do to the U.S. economy and the political process should unnerve American voters, including some Never Trumpers.
Harris fell into the top of the ticket when President Joe Biden dropped out of the race in July. Widely regarded as a political lightweight, the one-term California senator had hopes of becoming the 2020 Democratic presidential nominee but suspended her campaign before the primaries. She wasn't her party's first choice in 2024, but rather its last, desperate hope to save the White House.
Meanwhile, Harris's knowledge of politics and public policy is far too rudimentary for a potential occupant of the White House.
Here's her cringe-worthy, grade school-like response to a reporter in March 2022: "Ukraine is a country in Europe. It exists next to another country called Russia. Russia is a bigger country. Russia is a powerful country. Russia decided to invade a smaller country called Ukraine. So basically that's wrong."
She's also said: "We invested an additional $12 billion into community banks because we know community banks are in the community"; and "I think that, to be very honest with you, that I do believe that we should have rightly believed, but we certainly believe, that certain issues are just settled."
If she's a potential leader of the free world, then God help us all.
Harris' political ideology is also more left-wing than Biden's ever was on his worst day. She's pro-abortion, pro-affirmative action, supportive of strict gun-control legislation, a defender of sanctuary cities, believer in restrictive environmental protection laws and one of 10 senators to oppose the United States-Mexico-Canada Agreement because it didn't tackle climate change. She's a progressive's progressive.
Walz has tried to present himself as being slightly more moderate than Harris. He's certainly a better orator and political strategist than she'll ever be. Nevertheless, his political views and economic policies are similarly beyond the pale.
He signed the 2023 Protect Reproductive Options Act, which codified access to abortion in Minnesota. He decreed that Minnesota would be required to obtain its energy from wind, solar and various carbon-free sources by 2040. He provided free school meals to every child in his state, irrespective of income - a noble gesture, and an insanely expensive one. He's pro-union, pro-police reform and pro-marijuana legalization.
Walz has also made several bizarre, far-left statements. While working as a high school teacher in Nebraska in the 1990s, he reportedly said that China's communism system "means that everyone is the same and everyone shares ... the doctor and the construction worker make the same." More recently, he said that, "One person's socialism is another person's neighbourliness."
No one is suggesting Trump doesn't have his own political and personal deficiencies. They're a mile wide and an inch deep. Regardless, his nominal support for Republican and conservative values is far better than the Democratic alternative. Some of his biggest Never Trump critics likely know this deep down, too.
If Never Trumpers still regard themselves as free market thinkers and economic conservatives, they won't be able to vote for the Harris-Walz ticket. As much as they despise Trump, they know he wouldn't have a sympathetic ear to socialist rhetoric and support far-left policies that will tank the economy and ruin their nation for generations to come. Don't be surprised if some Never Trumpers bite their tongues and ultimately pull the lever to give the man they hate the second presidential term they wanted to avoid.
National Post
J.D. Tuccille: Harris's Deep State Democrats are oblivious to harms of over-regulation
Terry Newman: The Kamala Harris personality cult, an invention of liberal media

Document 366 of 500

Adam Pankratz: Wokeness is deservedly crashing. Let's be careful about retribution

Author: Carson Jerema

Publication info: Carson Jerema. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/adam-pankratz-wokeness-is-deservedly-crashing/docview/3096520780/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=Adam+Pankratz%3A+Wokeness+is+deservedly+crashing.+Let%27s+be+careful+about+retribution&amp;title=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-08-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Carson+Jerema&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: On Aug. 6, Ottawa's Capital Pride released a pro Palestinian statement which likely garnered considerably more attention than its authors imagined it would. The statement vaguely attempted to call out all intolerance, including antisemitism, but could not hide the fact that it was itself decidedly antisemitic, though that irony was obviously lost on Capital Pride.
The reaction from local Jewish groups was understandably swift in their declaration they would give Ottawa Pride a pass. A more surprising reaction came from quarters recently rather acquiescent or silent on many things antisemitic. Ottawa's mayor, the Ottawa-Carleton School board, local hospitals and even the Liberal Party of Canada withdrew from participation in the parade
The descent of Pride and other allegedly progressive events and institutions into the murky waters of extremist left and activist politics has been underway for a while now and is not unique to Capital Pride (see CUPE Ontario president Fred Hahn for example). So, in many ways, the journey of Capital Pride to the terminus of antisemitism and support for a region where being gay is punishable by death is not altogether surprising, sad though it may be.
But let us take a step back from this single event and look at the larger picture and the dangers very apparently lurking. Indeed, the reaction to the Capital Pride statement could well turn out to be the canary in the coal mine of backlash against activist extremism and woke politics.
For years it has been de rigueur for politicians of all stripes to participate in Pride. Justin Trudeau and the Liberals often act like they love nothing more than a good Pride march. That Capital Pride is too far gone for the Liberals, who have been especially mealy-mouthed on support for Israel following the Oct. 7 attacks, and who never miss an opportunity to burnish their progressive credentials, should raise eyebrows everywhere. In particular, it should seriously worry Capital Pride, as well as all "progressive" organizations which embraced the scourge of woke identity politics and allowed fringe elements within their organizations to take control.
However, this should also worry centrist and fair-minded Canadians. Large public organizations and politicians turning away from Pride and its newfound extremism may be a good thing, but that turn can quickly devolve into vicious backlash and retaliation if we are not collectively careful.
Some may argue that many of the organizations first in line for a backlash have had it coming for a while now. After all, these are the organizations and people who hounded anyone who said women couldn't have a penis out of a job, tarred innocent citizens as racist, who thought perhaps all lives did, in fact, matter, and generally created an environment of fear around the most salient cultural issues of the last decade. They deserve it, so goes this logic.
I do not disagree that there are people out there who, in the name of progress, displayed the worst and most vicious of behaviour towards anyone who did not agree with their extreme views. It is to be desired that they face consequences for having made the lives of others miserable or having cost blameless people their jobs. I, at any rate, will not shed a tear nor pity them if and when they finally met the sanction they deserve.
But in my desire to see the leaders of activist cancel culture brought to justice, I cannot help but look at the historical record of public retribution. It does not make for happy reading, as anyone who peruses French history circa 1794 can attest.
Retribution and backlash have never been precisely calibrated, scientific instruments. They are blunt tools, subject to wild swings and abuse by those who wield them. And, historically speaking, those who wield them tend to get a thrill out of revenge and reprisal.
This is the fear I have harboured for a while now: that the inevitable backlash against the insane and destructive scourge of activist identity politics would arrive and, when it came, the perpetrators would discover that they were a minority and, the majority now coming for them was not in a conciliatory mood. While minorities persecuting majorities is bad (as we have seen via cancel culture), a majority persecuting a minority, whatever they may have done, has the potential to be worse.
The most vehement and vocal adherents and actors in the culture wars of the past years have done enormous damage to both institutions and individuals. They have cost people their jobs, reputations and, in some cases, their lives It is not unnatural to want to see such bad actors harmed as they harmed others. By doing so, however, those of us who have stood against the tidal wave of woke activism which threatened society, risk becoming the beasts we fought so hard to push back. The Capital Pride debacle demonstrates the societal pendulum is swinging back, my fear is it will bludgeon indiscriminately and plunge us further into extreme societal divides.
National Post
Adam Pankratz is a lecturer at the University of British Columbia's Sauder School of Business.
John Robson: The progressive backlash against Capital Pride is something to behold
Amy Hamm: Pride tears itself apart over Israel, existence of gay conservatives

Document 367 of 500

'Experts'ignore rampant crime; Drug-injection site supporters disregard stats

Author: Finkle, Derek

Publication info: Finkle, Derek. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/expertsignore-rampant-crime-drug-injection-site/docview/3096432899/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=%27Experts%27ignore+rampant+crime%3B+Drug-injection+site+supporters+disregard+stats&amp;title=National+Post&amp;issn=14868008&amp;date=2024-08-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.9&amp;au=Finkle%2C+Derek&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The fallout from the announced closure of ten supervised injection sites in Ontario within 200 metres of schools and daycares on Tuesday was as swift as it was predictable.
Ontario Premier Doug Ford said the next day he was receiving an "endless" stream of calls from residents and business owners near one of those 10 sites slated to close, in the Leslieville neighbourhood of Toronto, where my family lives, to offer profuse gratitude. Meanwhile, harm-reduction activists launched into what they do best - calling people who disagree with them murderers.
These howls of murder and pending death sentences for the vulnerable aimed at the Ford government, which had just made the fateful decision, came from people as wildly diverse as associate professors of social work and the heads of nursing associations.
And it wasn't just Ford and his health minister, Sylvia Jones, who would be condemned to eternal damnation for supposedly orchestrating mass deaths by overdose. One of my neighbours, controversial Toronto writer/activist Desmond Cole, took time out from his ongoing efforts to free Palestine to hop on X to say "too many of my neighbours," all of whom thought our site was poorly located, will, by extension, have blood on our hands also.
Amid the accusations of bloodlust hurled at Leslieville parents growing weary of people threatening to burn their house down, rape their children or shoot someone's mother in cold blood, harm-reduction activists were taking a very wide detour around new injection site crime statistics unveiled as part of the health minister's announcement.
Multiple police departments and Ontario's solicitor general revealed that those who live close to injection sites report 113 per cent more assaults than neighbourhoods without them, not to mention 76 per cent more break and enters, 40 per cent more shootings, 97 per cent more robberies and 45 per cent more homicides.
While looking for something - anything - with which to splatter more blood on our provincial government, the CBC ran a story on Wednesday about how two reviews the Ontario Ministry of Health had commissioned last fall into the site I live across the street from, the South Riverdale Community Health Centre, had been "quietly posted online" the day of the health minister's big announcement.
The juicy hook in this story was that even though both reports recommended that the injection site in Leslieville remain open, albeit with a few recommended tweaks, the Ford government had dared to ignore the "expert" advice of dedicated health-care professionals.
Mike Crawley, who covers Ontario politics for the CBC, called the story a SCOOP (why is this always capitalized?), even though I had reported this very set of facts the night before in the Post.
When I first read the reports, both of which I had participated in to some extent, the word SCOOP wasn't what jumped to mind. I believe my initial thought was WAIT, THIS CAN'T BE. Followed by: IS THIS A JOKE? Then, at the end: WHERE'S THE REST? The main review into the South Riverdale site was conducted by Unity Health Toronto, a hospital network. From the very beginning, this review had been positioned by the ministry of health as a "critical incident report," something that typically emanates from an "unintended event" inside a health-care facility that results in death, disability or harm to a patient. In this case, weirdly, it was going to refer to a local mother of two named Karolina Huebner-Makurat who was fatally shot when two drug dealers outside the site allegedly tried to rob a third dealer and guns were drawn on July 7, 2023.
Most of my neighbours were sufficiently immersed in the breathtaking mismanagement of the South Riverdale site to be concerned when Unity Health was chosen to conduct the third-party review in October. A branch of Unity Health, MAP Centre for Urban Health Solutions, was instrumental in conducting the research that laid the foundation for the first supervised injection sites to open in Toronto in 2017. We'd expected something a little more arm's-length.
We also expected any review to provide a deep dive into the currently unknown details about the death of Huebner-Makurat - the critical incident in question. What was the relationship, if any, between the centre and the alleged shooters? Why was one of the alleged shooters seen entering South Riverdale the day of the shooting? Had the site's employees expressed concerns about drug dealers outside the site? In layman's terms, we wanted answers. We were sorely disappointed. One of the injection-site employees, Khalila Mohammed, was charged a little more than a month after Huebner-Makurat's death with aiding and abetting one of the alleged shooters. Guess what? Mohammed's name is nowhere to be found in any of the reports.
Those most impacted by the site's unintended but foreseeable consequences also expected the report to provide some kind of explanation for why leadership of the site ignored the many safety concerns of its neighbours, concerns that stretched back years prior to the shooting. Why was the site's relationship with police "broken," according to the local superintendent after the shooting? Beyond answers, we naively hoped some form of accountability would present itself.
Instead, the Unity Health report authors, including Dr. Ahmed Bayoumi, the godfather of supervised injection in Toronto, produced what one of my neighbours accurately calls a "community satisfaction survey as filtered through the self-styled lens of public health."
Even the report by Jill Campbell, the provincially appointed supervisor who oversaw the site for six months was, from an accountability standpoint, a monumental disappointment (except for the part in which she disclosed that when harm-reduction employees use the word "community," they mean drug users and clients only). I met with Campbell, both virtually and in person, a handful of times between December and April. Campbell openly acknowledged to me and other neighbours the mismanagement of the centre and the profound mistrust it created with the surrounding community.
Her report, however, begins by wondering aloud how marginalized people are going to pay for coffee at the café across the street from the site that doesn't accept cash (partly as a result of rampant drug-fuelled theft), quotes Mahatma Gandhi and chides residents with security cameras for violating some vague city privacy bylaw because they'd sent site management photographs of clients buying and using drugs on South Riverdale property, never acknowledging that homicide detectives used footage from these same security cameras to identify the wanted shooters.
Just days before I met Campbell for the first time in December, the National Post had published a feature-length story by Tara Riley, a harm-reduction employee at South Riverdale for six years. Riley's account included stunning revelations: that drug dealers had quickly wormed their way inside the site after it first opened to sell to clients from the comfort of the injection site booths and that staff with "lived experience" regularly used drugs and sometimes overdosed at work. Even though Campbell met with Riley in person, Riley's name and shocking allegations are nowhere to be found in Campbell's report to the ministry.
While Campbell bizarrely framed our neighbourhood's criticisms of the site as a communications shortcoming above all else, the same cannot be said for Unity Health's Community Engagement Report, a companion document to the main review. Here, Unity Health had no choice but to concede that "most respondents felt strongly that the (site) should be closed or moved to a different location."
Without alerting readers to its own self-interest in the continued existence of the very sites for which it planted and watered the seeds years ago, Unity Health clarified that this, my neighbourhood's most desired change, conveniently quarantined in a separate document, would not be included in the recommendations of its main report. No wonder Doug Ford took a pass.
National Post

Document 368 of 500

Union finally does the right thing after a whole lot of wrong

Author: Ivison, John

Publication info: Ivison, John. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/union-finally-does-right-thing-after-whole-lot/docview/3096430128/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Apq1busgeneral&amp;atitle=Union+finally+does+the+right+thing+after+a+whole+lot+of+wrong&amp;title=National+Post&amp;issn=14868008&amp;date=2024-08-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=Ivison%2C+John&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=National+Post&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The news that the national board of the Canadian Public Employees Union has called on its vice-president, Fred Hahn, to step down, after posting an antisemitic video, feels like a tipping point.
The union said in a release that it has lost confidence in Hahn, who is also president of its political wing, CUPE Ontario, for posting a digitally manipulated video that showed a Jewish Olympic athlete leaping off a diving board and turning into a bomb.
Hahn has claimed he has "never celebrated violence," even though he wrote "resistance brings progress."
His apparent ouster is a case of CUPE doing the right thing only after they have tried everything else. The union has backed Hahn to the hilt in the human rights complaint launched by 70 Jewish union members who say they have been "scared, silenced, discriminated, threatened and harassed" by CUPE's reaction to the October 7 assault on Israel by Hamas.
A day after the attack, at a time when Jews around the world were emotionally shattered, Hahn praised "the power of resistance" on social media. Two weeks later, he apologized - as he has done in the case of the video - but in both cases, expediency, rather than conviction, seem to have been the motivation.
CUPE not only supported Hahn, it claimed the union was merely engaging in a disagreement over politics, putting it beyond the jurisdiction of the Ontario human rights code. Nowhere did it explain why a public sector union is so obsessed with Israel's destruction, far less why it is comfortable with discriminating against a minority of its own members. The admission that Hahn was in the wrong may prove costly - the complainants are seeking $500,000 in damages.
More broadly, CUPE's statement has the sense of a moment in time when the illiberal progressives who have dominated the public square for the last decade have been forced to acknowledge that they have steered so far in the opposite direction from the prevailing public mood that their position is no longer tenable.
It is a typically understated Canadian milestone - neither as brash as Bud Light's fall from its perch as America's top-selling beer after partnering with a transgender influencer, nor as weighty as the British response to an expert pediatric report that reviewed the treatment of transgender children and found that the majority should not manage gender-related distress with puberty-blockers or surgery.
But, in common with both those examples, Hahn's disavowal by his own employer suggests the tide has well and truly turned.
Like the Liberal party's decision not to take in the Ottawa Capital Pride parade after its organizers pledged support for Palestinians and accused critics of "pink-washing" the war in Gaza, it seems to be an acknowledgment that the median voter is mad as hell and is not going to take it anymore.
The illiberal left has been unabashed about its intention to impose equality and "justice" for those deemed to be oppressed identity groups. Individual rights have been trampled and opponents bullied and cancelled.
Rather than setting fair conditions and allowing outcomes to unfold through competition, playing fields have been tilted to favour particular groups.
American economist Milton Friedman once said that the society that puts equality before freedom will end up with neither. He has been proven prophetic (again).
Ideological zealots in government, media and civil society have imposed an agenda that has divided Western democracies into antagonistic tribes.
Business has quietly moved away from embracing this censorious, self-anointed vision. Diversity, equity and inclusion promises have not been kept and companies have publicly acknowledged that appearing to be "woke" is not good business.
Disney chief Bob Iger said that thrusting progressive social messages down the throats of moviegoers was bad box office. "We have to entertain first," he said.
It's proven to be bad politics too. As I pointed out here, the Trudeau government's hubris that it alone had the blueprint to deliver sexual, racial, social and environmental justice is in large measure why it is now 20 points behind in the polls, trailing the Conservatives in every region, in every age group, with both men and women and on every major issue except climate change. Simply put, they left the median voter behind, convinced that they knew best.
Whether it was the Manson murders, the stabbing at the Rolling Stones concert in Altamont or the day they started selling hippie wigs in Woolworths, the '60s ended.
Arguably, October 7 put the nail in the coffin in the era of the illiberal left's tyranny.
Progressives justified the murder of the innocents because privileged "white" Jews could not be seen as victims. But it exposed the contradictions inherent in identifying "the oppressed" and preferred outcomes.
The Guardian's Gaby Hinsliff argues that "woke" hasn't peaked or run its course; rather, it has entered the mainstream and its goals are now those of the establishment.
But Western liberalism has long embraced incremental change and justice for all, regardless of sex, class or race.
This recent phenomenon was something else, decrying debate or common-sense reform. It granted people like the kaffiyeh-clad Hahn a literal bullhorn to tell demonstrators: "We will live to see the end of apartheid Israel" and to claim the University of Toronto had no right to eject protests from private property on the basis that the U of T campus is "stolen land."
I admit a degree of sober satisfaction at his downfall.
National Post jivison@criffel.ca Twitter.com/IvisonJ
